skill.name: run-train
skill.description: Conservative training command executor for deep learning research repositories. Captures startup verification, short-run verification, full kickoff, or resume runs with structured evidence in train_outputs/.
skill.summary: Execute a selected training command conservatively and write standardized training evidence to train_outputs/.
skill.author: lllllllama

security_audit.summary: All 29 static findings evaluated and dismissed as false positives. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' detections trigger on natural language (hash, summary) in YAML/Markdown, not on actual crypto. The 'shell backtick' detections are markdown code formatting characters. The 'path traversal' detections are documentation path references (../../references/...). The 'subprocess.run' usages are intentional and safe: git commands use hardcoded arguments, and the training command executor uses shlex.split() for safe argument parsing with timeouts and output capture. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, or malicious intent detected.

low_findings.title: Subprocess execution of user-selected training command
low_findings.description: execute_command() runs the user-selected training command via subprocess.run with shlex.split() parsing. The command is the core input of the skill; timeouts, capture_output, and git status diffs are used as safeguards. This is by design for a training executor skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly shows subprocess.run with shlex.split on user-provided command, but with proper timeout and output capture. Intent is legitimate training execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static false positive: subprocess.run flagged
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged subprocess.run at scripts/run_training.py:72 and :164. The line 72 usage calls hardcoded git commands ('rev-parse', 'status') with no injection vector. The line 164 usage runs the user-selected training command which is the explicit purpose of the skill. Both use shlex.split() for safe argument parsing and capture_output=True for safety.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both subprocess calls are clearly safe: hardcoded args for git, and shlex-parsed training command with timeout for the executor. No injection risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static false positive: markdown backticks flagged as shell execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged markdown code formatting (backtick characters in SKILL.md and training-policy.md) as Ruby/shell backtick execution. These are standard markdown inline code formatting, not shell command substitution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Backticks in markdown files are formatting characters. No shell interpretation occurs in .md files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static false positive: path traversal flagged in documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged ../../ path references in SKILL.md as path traversal. These are relative documentation references to shared skill files (e.g., ../../references/agent-operating-principles.md), not filesystem access patterns.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Relative path references in markdown documentation are standard cross-references, not path traversal attacks.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static false positive: weak cryptographic algorithm flagged
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged words like 'hash', 'summary', and 'CHECKPOINT' as weak cryptographic algorithms. These are natural language terms in YAML descriptions and Python variable names (e.g., checkpoint_candidates list, argparse help text). No actual cryptographic operations are performed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: No hash functions or crypto code found. All matches are natural language in descriptions and variable names.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static false positive: system reconnaissance flagged
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged training-policy.md:19 as system reconnaissance. The line is part of a markdown '## Avoid' section listing items to avoid (exploratory sweeps, speculative architecture changes). It is documentation, not code execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Markdown documentation listing anti-patterns. No system calls or reconnaissance code present.

content.user_title: Run Training Commands with Structured Evidence
content.value_statement: Deep learning training runs often lack reproducible evidence and clear status reporting. This skill executes a selected training command conservatively and writes standardized training evidence to train_outputs/.
actual_capabilities: Execute a user-selected training command with configurable timeout
Capture startup verification, short-run verification, full kickoff, and resume run modes
Parse training output to extract epoch, step, checkpoint, and metric progress
Write structured evidence files to train_outputs/ (SUMMARY.md, COMMANDS.md, LOG.md, status.json)
Snapshot git status before and after execution to track changed files
Classify outcomes as blocked, partial, success, or resumed with stop reasons
limitations: Does not perform environment setup or asset download
Does not run exploratory sweeps or multi-variant experiments
Does not choose the overall research goal or architecture
Monitoring is bounded by the configured timeout window

use_cases.title: Startup verification of a training pipeline
use_cases.description: Run a training command for a short window to confirm it launches without errors before committing to a full run.
use_cases.target_user: ML researchers validating a new training script

use_cases.title: Resume from checkpoint with evidence
use_cases.description: Continue training from a saved checkpoint and record the resume event, completed steps, and best metric.
use_cases.target_user: Engineers resuming interrupted long training jobs

use_cases.title: Conservative full kickoff with status reporting
use_cases.description: Launch a full training run within a monitoring window and capture structured status and log evidence.
use_cases.target_user: Research leads needing reproducible run records

prompt_templates.title: Startup verification
prompt_templates.prompt: Run startup verification for the training command `python train.py --config configs/exp1.yaml` in repo `/home/user/project` with a 60-second monitoring window.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quickly check that a training script launches correctly

prompt_templates.title: Short-run verification
prompt_templates.prompt: Execute a short-run verification of `python train.py --config configs/exp2.yaml` for 120 seconds and report the first checkpoint and loss curve.
prompt_templates.scenario: Validate training progress over a brief window

prompt_templates.title: Resume from checkpoint
prompt_templates.prompt: Resume training from `checkpoints/exp3_step5000.ckpt` using `python train.py --resume checkpoints/exp3_step5000.ckpt` in resume mode.
prompt_templates.scenario: Continue an interrupted training run from a saved checkpoint

prompt_templates.title: Full kickoff with evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Launch the full training kickoff for `python train.py --config configs/final.yaml` with a 600-second monitoring window and write all evidence to train_outputs/.
prompt_templates.scenario: Start a full training run and capture complete run records

output_examples.input: Run startup verification for `python train.py --config configs/exp1.yaml` with 60s timeout
output: train_outputs/SUMMARY.md — run summary with status, completed steps, best metric
train_outputs/COMMANDS.md — exact command executed and arguments
train_outputs/LOG.md — captured stdout and stderr from the run
train_outputs/status.json — machine-readable status with stop_reason and evidence_capture

output_examples.input: Resume from checkpoints/exp3_step5000.ckpt in resume mode
Launch full training kickoff with 600s monitoring window
output: evidence_capture: git-status-diff with before/after file change tracking

best_practices: Always state the run mode explicitly (startup verification, short-run, full kickoff, or resume) before launching
Set a monitoring timeout that matches the expected early-progress window for your experiment
Review the git status diff in the evidence output to confirm only expected files were modified
Use the trusted lane for verified training commands and explore lane for experimental runs
anti_patterns: Using this skill for environment setup, dependency installation, or asset download
Running exploratory sweeps or multi-variant experiments through this skill
Treating startup verification results as evidence of full reproduction success
Invoking speculative architecture changes or autonomous idea implementation through this skill

faq.question: What run modes does this skill support?
faq.answer: The skill supports startup verification, short-run verification, full kickoff, and resume modes, each with its own stop reason classification.

faq.question: Where are the training evidence files written?
faq.answer: All evidence is written to the train_outputs/ directory inside the target repository, including SUMMARY.md, COMMANDS.md, LOG.md, SCIENTIFIC_CHANGELOG.md, COMPARABILITY_REPORT.md, and status.json.

faq.question: How does the skill track file changes during a run?
faq.answer: The skill captures git status before and after execution and computes changed, new, and deleted files to include in the evidence bundle.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for exploratory hyperparameter sweeps?
faq.answer: No. The skill is designed for conservative execution of a single selected training command. Exploratory sweeps should use a dedicated experiment planning skill.

faq.question: What happens if the training command exceeds the monitoring window?
faq.answer: The subprocess is terminated at the timeout, and the run is classified as partial with stop_reason set to monitoring_window_elapsed or startup_verification_window_elapsed.

faq.question: Does this skill install dependencies or download datasets?
faq.answer: No. The skill only executes the selected training command. Environment setup and asset download are explicitly out of scope and should be handled before invoking this skill.
